5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image Generated Using AI“The same technology. The same country. Two completely different outcomes; one generated laughter, the other caused unimaginable harm.” Earlier this week, a short video from what appeared to be a wedding ceremony began circulating online.
During his speech, one of the guests accidentally combined the names of two of South Sudan’s vice presidents into one. In a country with five vice presidents, the mistake was amusing enough to attract attention. But that is not the point of this story. Within hours, the clip had spread across social media. As often happens today, someone decided to take the joke a step further. Using generative AI, they created an image of an imaginary vice president by combining the faces and names of the two officials.
Judging from the result, the prompt likely referenced existing photographs of both leaders to generate a fictional character. It was humorous. It appeared harmless. Many people laughed.
But as debates around artificial intelligence continue to evolve, I found myself asking a simple but increasingly important question: How ethical was it?
Just a few days earlier, on 2 July 2026, I had the privilege of moderating a panel discussion on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technologies during the South Sudan Internet Governance Forum (SSIGF 2026). Our discussion focused on AI governance in emerging contexts such as South Sudan.
One conversation from that panel has stayed with me ever since.While discussing the ethical implications of AI, one of the panelists, an academic, shared a deeply disturbing story.
About six weeks earlier, a woman had told her that her son had taken his own life after enduring relentless bullying. His classmates had used generative AI to create humiliating images of him and repeatedly sent them to him. The sustained abuse pushed him into depression, and ultimately, he died by suicide.
These are two stories from the same country. The same technology.The same class of AI tools. Yet they produced completely different outcomes. One generated laughter. The other caused unimaginable harm. That contrast is what continues to occupy my mind.
Beyond the immediate tragedy, incidents like these expose a much deeper challenge. Harmful uses of generative AI can create fear and distrust, discouraging people from embracing technologies that could otherwise improve lives. In countries already facing digital inequalities, this fear risks widening the digital divide even further.
For fragile societies like South Sudan, where communities continue to grapple with histories of communal violence, revenge killings, and political tensions, the stakes are even higher. AI-generated misinformation, manipulated images, and synthetic media have the potential to inflame existing tensions in ways that are difficult to predict or contain.
These realities raise difficult governance questions. Who should be held accountable when AI causes harm? Is it the individual who created the content? The company that built the model? The platform that allowed it to spread? The manufacturer of the device? Or the regulator responsible for oversight?
As I reflected on these questions, I remembered another incident from earlier this year involving a senior female government official whose image was maliciously manipulated using generative AI and then circulated online.
Different victims.Different contexts. The same underlying challenge. Together, these stories illustrate both the extraordinary promise and the profound risks of artificial intelligence. Searching for a framework to think through these experiences, I revisited AI Ethics by philosopher Mark Coeckelbergh.
One idea particularly resonated with me: the tension between viewing AI as a tool that advances humanity and fearing a future where humans become increasingly subordinate to the technologies they create.
This tension sits at the heart of contemporary AI ethics. There is no doubt that artificial intelligence is already transforming the way we live and work. AI increasingly influences decision-making in sectors ranging from healthcare and education to finance, justice, and public administration.
On a personal level, I use AI-powered tools almost every day to support research, writing, planning, and productivity. They have undoubtedly made me more efficient. Yet these benefits do not eliminate the ethical questions. Instead, they make them more urgent. Concerns around privacy, bias, discrimination, transparency, safety, accountability, and misinformation are no longer theoretical debates confined to academic journals. They are becoming everyday realities.
As AI rapidly reshapes societies, we must ask who benefits, who bears the risks, and who ultimately holds power over these technologies. If AI generated abuse contributes to someone’s death, where does accountability begin, and where does it end?
Should responsibility rest solely with the individual who misused the technology? Do AI developers have obligations beyond building the systems? What responsibility do social media platforms have for amplifying harmful content? What role should governments and regulators play? These questions become even more complex when we consider that the development of advanced AI technologies remains concentrated among a relatively small number of global companies.
This concentration raises important concerns about equity, fairness, and whose values are ultimately embedded within these systems. But perhaps the most important questions are not technological at all. They are deeply human. As consumers of AI, how do we redefine our values in an age where machines can generate convincing images, voices, videos, and text within seconds?
What becomes socially acceptable? What should remain unacceptable, regardless of technological capability?
How do we teach ethical AI use in societies where digital literacy remains uneven and where many people are only beginning their journey into the digital world?
Do we need stronger laws?
Better education? Clearer ethical guidelines?
Or perhaps all three?
As societies become increasingly dependent on artificial intelligence, ethical questions cease to be merely technical concerns. They become questions about human vulnerability, trust, justice, and dignity. The more AI becomes embedded in our daily lives, the more its ethical implications become inseparable from our own.
That is why I believe conversations about AI ethics must move beyond international conferences, research institutions, and technology companies. They must become local conversations, held in classrooms, communities, homes, universities, boardrooms, and government institutions. We need to examine these questions through our own cultural, legal, and social realities.
What does responsible AI look like in South Sudan? Which existing laws already apply?
Where do the gaps exist?
How should our education system prepare young people not only to build AI but also to use it responsibly?
The future of AI governance will not be shaped solely by engineers or policymakers. It will also be shaped by ordinary citizens making ethical choices every day. The stories we are witnessing today should not simply entertain or alarm us. They should challenge us. Because the real question is no longer whether AI will transform our society. It already is.
The question is whether our ethics, institutions, and values will evolve quickly enough to guide that transformation toward human flourishing rather than human harm.
